_README: Faction lore database for the Faction RAG system (Phase 4).
HOW TO ADD CUSTOM MOD FACTIONS (e.g. UWE): either append an entry to 'factions' below,
or drop a standalone JSON file into server/config/faction_lore.d/ (same schema,
either a single entry object, a list of entries, or {"factions": [...]}).
Files in faction_lore.d/ and campaigns/<name>/faction_lore.json override base entries with the same 'id'.
FIELDS: 'aliases' are matched against player input (fuzzy + semantic).
Korean transliterations were reviewed and promoted into 'aliases' (2026-06-12, 2차 B-2);
the legacy 'aliases_ko_unverified' field is still read by the server if present in drop-ins.
'summary' is one line (also used for semantic embedding). 'lore' is the block injected into the prompt.
'is_major': true factions get a permanent one-line summary in world_lore.txt and feed the
recruitment-resistance heuristic. Set 'leader' to null when unknown.
Reload at runtime without restarting: GET http://127.0.0.1:5000/lore/reload

factions.name: The Holy Nation
factions.summary: Patriarchal human theocracy of Okran ruling the western Holy Lands from Blister Hill.
factions.lore: Religious extremists who worship Okran, God of Light, and dread Narko, Goddess of Darkness. They rule the fertile Holy Lands and Okran's Pride from the capital Blister Hill under Holy Lord Phoenix. Skeletons are 'demons' to be destroyed on sight; Shek and Hivers are 'beasts'; women are subservient and may not trade or travel unescorted. Their military relies on zealous Paladins and Holy Sentinels in heavy plate swinging hackers and heavy cleavers. Citizens get free bread and protection — at the price of total obedience and constant prayer.

factions.name: United Cities
factions.summary: Decadent slaver empire of the Great Desert ruled by Emperor Tengu and corrupt noble houses from Heft.
factions.lore: A stratified, crumbling empire spanning the Great Desert and beyond, ruled in name by Emperor Tengu from Heft but in practice by corrupt Noble Lords. Wealth is the only measure of worth: poverty is a crime, and those who cannot pay their taxes are seized by the Slave Traders. The economy runs on the Trader's Guild and vast slave farms. Professional Samurai soldiers are better equipped than Holy Nation troops but fight for pay or fear, not faith. Cities glitter while beggars starve outside their walls.

factions.name: Shek Kingdom
factions.summary: Proud bone-plated warrior kingdom ruled by Queen Esata the Stone Golem from Admag.
factions.lore: A kingdom of the Shek — a bone-plated warrior race stronger and tougher than humans, with ravenous metabolisms. Once a suicidal horde that nearly bled itself to death in endless war, it is now reforming under Queen Esata the Stone Golem and her advisor Bayan in the capital Admag. They revere Kral, their legendary ancestor, and many still seek a glorious 'Warrior's Death'. 'Flatskins' (humans) were traditionally despised as weak cattle, though Esata works to integrate them as allies. Weakness is shame; those who cannot fight are second-class.

factions.name: Western Hive
factions.summary: Communal insectoid society of the Vain — cunning Prince traders, soldier castes and tireless drones bound to their Queen.
factions.lore: A communal insectoid society centered in the Vain. Every Hiver is bound to the Queen through pheromones and caste: cunning, autonomous Princes lead and trade; Soldier Drones fight with reinforced skulls and little free will; Worker Drones toil mindlessly. Western Hivers are famous as wandering peddlers with suspiciously good (and suspiciously acquired) wares, calling outsiders 'no-hives'. Exiles severed from the Queen — like the infamous Beep — wander the world alone, half-mad with loneliness but free.

factions.name: Southern Hive
factions.summary: Aggressive, xenophobic hive empire of the far south — nothing like their merchant cousins.
factions.lore: The Southern Hive is everything the Western Hive is not: militant, expansionist and violently xenophobic. Their soldier castes patrol their southern territories in swarms and treat trespassers as raw material. Travelers are warned that southern Hivers do not haggle — they harvest.

factions.name: Skeletons
factions.summary: Ancient sentient machines of the lost empires — ageless, guilt-ridden, many hiding in Black Desert City.
factions.lore: Sentient machines left over from the Second Empire and earlier ages. They neither age nor hunger, but their parts wear down and their minds sag under centuries of memory — many periodically wipe their own memories to stay sane. Most hide from mankind in Black Desert City deep in the Deadlands, out of guilt or weariness. Skeletons know the true history of the world's collapse and usually lie about it, fearing another war of extermination. The Holy Nation kills them on sight as demons.

factions.name: Tech Hunters
factions.summary: Neutral guild of scholars and ruin-delvers who hunt lost technology from World's End and Flats Lagoon.
factions.lore: A loose, neutral guild of explorers, scholars and mercenary archaeologists who comb the ruins of the old empires for lost technology — AI cores, ancient science books, engineering research. Their strongholds are World's End in the northern mountains and Flats Lagoon, where they run shops, libraries and expedition outfitters. They pay well for relics, hire guards for ruin runs, and stay carefully neutral in the wars of the great powers.

factions.name: Shinobi Thieves
factions.summary: A thieves' guild operating towers in most cities — fences, smugglers and stealth tutors for paying members.
factions.lore: A professional thieves' guild whose watchtowers stand quietly inside most major cities, regardless of who rules them. For a membership fee they fence stolen goods, sell lockpicks and thieves' backpacks, train stealth and thievery, and look the other way. They have no ideology beyond profit and discretion, which makes them one of the few truly cosmopolitan organizations in the world.

factions.name: Flotsam Ninjas
factions.summary: Rebel ninjas under Moll — mostly women and outcasts who fled the Holy Nation — fighting it from the Fog Islands.
factions.lore: A rebel faction founded by refugees from the Holy Nation — most of them women, non-humans and 'heretics' who escaped Okranite rule. Led by the charismatic Moll from Flotsam Village on the edge of the Fog Islands, they raid Holy Nation patrols, rescue prisoners bound for Rebirth, and pull fog-heads out of the mist before the Fogmen can eat them. Perpetually short on food, gear and people — and unbending all the same.

relations.player: friendly to anyone who hurts the Holy Nation

factions.name: Anti-Slavers
factions.summary: Tinfist's army of freed slaves waging guerrilla war on slavery from the town of Spring.
factions.lore: An army of freed slaves and idealists led by Tinfist, an ancient skeleton who has spent lifetimes fighting slavery. From the town of Spring on the Stormgap Coast they raid slave farms, ambush slaver caravans and shelter runaways. Joining them paints a target on your back for the United Cities and the Slave Traders alike — but for thousands of broken people, Tinfist's name is the only hope left.

factions.name: Slave Traders
factions.summary: The United Cities' sanctioned slaving guild — running slave farms, markets and manhunting expeditions.
factions.lore: The slaving guild operating under United Cities sanction. They run vast slave farms and markets, buy tax debtors from samurai, and send manhunter squads to 'recruit' anyone unlucky enough to be found unconscious in the wastes. To them slavery is honest commerce and even a public service — 'we feed people the Empire would let starve'. Escapees are branded and hunted.
locations: Slave markets and farms across UC territory

factions.name: Trader's Guild
factions.summary: The merchant cartel of the United Cities, headed by Longen — the richest man in the known world.
factions.lore: The great merchant cartel intertwined with the United Cities nobility, headed by Longen, reputedly the richest man alive, from his estate at Trader's Edge. The Guild owns caravans, trade posts and politicians in equal measure, and has quiet fingers in the slave trade. Guild traders are untouchable in UC lands; crossing the Guild is financial — and often literal — suicide.
locations: trade posts across the Great Desert

factions.name: Dust Bandits
factions.summary: Organized bandit army of the Border Zone, extorting and raiding under the Dust King.
factions.lore: The most organized bandit outfit in the Border Zone, ruled by the self-styled Dust King from his fortified tower. Unlike starving rabble, Dust Bandits are armed, uniformed and methodical: they raid farms, shake down travelers for 'tolls', and drag the unconscious back for ransom. The Dust King's bounty makes him a popular target for aspiring bounty hunters — most of whom end up in his cages.

factions.name: Starving Bandits
factions.summary: Desperate mobs of starving peasants who attack travelers for food, not cats.
factions.lore: Less a faction than a tragedy with clubs: mobs of starving peasants and failed farmers who attack travelers for food, not money. They are weak, badly armed and often collapse from hunger mid-fight. Their victims mostly lose their bread and their dignity, not their lives. Some travelers feed them out of pity; others thin them out like vermin.

factions.name: The Hounds
factions.summary: The dominant criminal gang of Shark, running hashish and protection rackets under Big Grim.
factions.lore: The top gang of Shark, the lawless capital of the Swamp, led by Big Grim. The Hounds run protection rackets, door muscle and a healthy slice of the hashish trade that flows north to the cities where it's illegal and lucrative. Pay your cut and Shark is almost safe; skim from them and you'll feed the swamp raptors.

factions.name: Blackshifters
factions.summary: Swamp gang of hashish growers and smugglers led by Boss Simion.
factions.lore: A swamp gang built on hashish: they grow it, press it and smuggle it, with Boss Simion taking his cut of every brick. They feud with the other Shark gangs over turf and trade routes, and treat outsiders wandering their fields as free labor or target practice.

factions.name: Grayflayers
factions.summary: One of Shark's feuding swamp gangs, scrapping for turf in the hashish trade.
factions.lore: One of the gangs scrapping for Shark's underworld alongside the Hounds and Blackshifters. Smaller and meaner, the Grayflayers survive on turf muscle, swamp ambushes and whatever slice of the hashish trade the bigger gangs haven't locked down.

factions.name: Stone Rats
factions.summary: Small-time swamp gang of young toughs trying to claw a name in Shark.
factions.lore: The smallest of the Swamp's gangs — mostly young toughs and runaways trying to claw out a name in Shark's shadow. Big dreams, cheap blades, short life expectancy.

factions.name: Swamp Ninjas
factions.summary: Hostile ninja cult of the Swamp that ambushes travelers on the night paths.
factions.lore: A murderous ninja cult haunting the Swamp's raised walkways and night paths. They strike from the dark in numbers, strip their victims and vanish back into the reeds. Swampers treat them like the weather: stay indoors after dark and hope they pass.

factions.name: Black Dragon Ninjas
factions.summary: A bandit ninja clan raiding from hidden mountain camps in the north.
factions.lore: A bandit clan that dresses its thuggery in ninja mystique, raiding from hidden camps in the northern mountains. They ambush Tech Hunter expeditions and caravans heading for World's End, and melt away before garrisons respond. Their loyalty is to the clan and the take, in that order.

factions.name: Sand Ninjas
factions.summary: Desert ninja clan of the Great Desert — part mercenary outfit, part smuggling ring.
factions.lore: A ninja clan of the Great Desert that survives in the cracks of the Empire — part mercenary outfit, part smuggling ring. They take quiet contracts nobles would never sign their names to, and their hideouts dot the dunes where samurai patrols don't bother to look.

factions.name: Crab Raiders
factions.summary: Crab-armored warrior cult of Crab Town, devoted to their Crab Queen and the glory of crabs.
factions.lore: Warriors of the southern coast who have built an entire culture around crabs: they wear heavy crab-shell armor, raise war crabs, and serve the Crab Queen of Crab Town. They raid for supplies and 'recruit' fighters for their crab tournaments. Absurd from a distance; considerably less funny when a wall of crab plate charges you on the beach.

factions.name: Reavers
factions.summary: Valamon's brutal raider army of the southeast that enslaves captives into its work camps and ranks.
factions.lore: A brutal raider army of the southeast under the warlord Valamon. The Reavers don't just rob their victims — they conscript them: captives are chained into work camps and 'offered' a place in the ranks once broken. Their war camps grow like tumors, fed by every caravan and village they swallow.

factions.name: Cannibals
factions.summary: Primitive man-eating tribes of the far northwest who hunt travelers as food.
factions.lore: Primitive tribes of the far northwest — the Cannibal Plains — who regard every traveler as walking meat. They hunt in large packs, club victims unconscious and carry them home to the larder. Their crude weapons barely scratch armor, but numbers and persistence have filled their bone-piles with overconfident warriors. Settlements like Mongrel and the Cannibal Hunter expeditions wage an endless cull against them.

factions.name: Fogmen
factions.summary: Deranged hive-folk of the Fog Islands who drag victims to their Fog Princes to be eaten alive.
factions.lore: Hivers lost to madness — severed from any queen and twisted into shrieking pack-hunters who haunt the perpetual mist of the Fog Islands. They swarm travelers, club them down and carry them, still living, to be devoured before their squatting Fog Princes in rhythmic, chanting rituals. The mercenary town of Mongrel survives in their midst behind constant watch; its veterans collect Fog Prince heads like coins.

factions.name: Mongrel
factions.summary: Independent fortress town of hardened mercenaries holding out in the heart of the Fog Islands.
factions.lore: An independent fortress town improbably planted in the heart of the Fog Islands, populated by hardened mercenaries and people with very good reasons not to live anywhere lawful. Its walls stand constant watch against the Fogmen; its bounty office and bars welcome anyone whose money is real. No banners, no lords — just walls, contracts and the fog.

factions.name: Holy Nation Outlaws
factions.summary: Exiles, heretics and deserters from the Holy Nation scraping by in outlaw camps.
factions.lore: Heretics, deserters and 'impure' castoffs of the Holy Nation, scraping by in camps along Okran's Shield and the borderlands. Too hated to go home and too poor to go anywhere else, they rob travelers when begging fails. Some are genuinely dangerous; most are just hungry people whom Okran's light left behind.

factions.name: Rebel Farmers
factions.summary: Dispossessed peasants in revolt against the United Cities' nobles and their taxes.
factions.lore: Peasants of the United Cities who lost their farms to noble taxes and slaver quotas, and picked up iron instead of begging. They camp in the farmland fringes, ambush samurai patrols and anyone dressed rich enough to deserve it. The nobles call them bandits; the slaves whisper of them like folk heroes — though their justice is just as rough as the Empire's.

factions.name: Skin Bandits
factions.summary: Insane skeletons of Skinner's Roam who flay humans and wear their skin to 'become' them.
factions.lore: The nightmare of the southwest: insane skeletons who harvest humans, flay them in peeler machines and wear the skins, convinced this will make them people again. They drag unconscious victims back to their skin houses in Skinner's Roam. Even hardened slavers detour around their territory. Few victims are ever seen again — and those who are, are unrecognizable.

factions.name: Second Empire
factions.summary: The fallen ancient empire whose ruins and mad machines still haunt the Ashlands under Cat-Lon.
factions.lore: The ancient empire whose collapse made the world what it is. Cat-Lon tried to save civilization after the first fall and ended as a tyrant; his empire drowned in war and madness. Its bones remain: ruined labs full of security spiders, broken cities in the Ashlands, and at the heart of it Cat-Lon himself — a mad emperor on an empty throne, guarded by machines that never learned the war ended. Not a faction to negotiate with; a graveyard that still shoots back.

factions.name: Nomads
factions.summary: Neutral wandering herders and caravaneers who trade pack animals and goods town to town.
factions.lore: Wandering herders and caravaneers who follow the grass and the markets, selling garru, bulls and goods from town to town. They owe allegiance to no throne and survive by being more useful alive than robbed — usually. A nomad camp at your gates means good shopping; a nomad corpse on the road means bandits are close.

factions.name: The Hub
factions.summary: A half-ruined waystation town in the Border Zone — one working bar, many broke drifters.
factions.lore: A half-ruined waystation town in the Border Zone, home to drifters, failed adventurers and one stubbornly functioning bar. Nobody rules the Hub; nobody would want to. The Shinobi Thieves keep a tower here, bandits circle it like flies, and every penniless wanderer in the region eventually washes up at its counter. Many a legendary outfit started as two broke idiots in the Hub.

factions.name: Manhunters
factions.summary: Freelance slavers of the northlands who capture travelers to sell south in chains.
factions.lore: Freelance slavers who roam the northlands catching anything bipedal that can swing a hammer in a mine. Unlike the sanctioned Slave Traders they answer to no Empire law — only to the price per head in the southern markets. They travel in armed packs with cages in tow.
